title: Get Something Working
tagline: Build real pages from your very first line of code.

lessons.title: Make Your Name Appear in a Browser

accomplishments: Make text appear in a web browser
See your name displayed as a big heading

do.title: Create your first web page
steps: Open VS Code (or download it free from code.visualstudio.com if you don't have it)
Save it to your Desktop (so you can find it easily)
do.code:
Hello! I'm [YOUR NAME]

I just created my first web page!
do.result: You just created a working web page with HTML!
do.proofRequired: your web page in the browser with YOUR name displayed

concepts.name: HTML (HyperText Markup Language)
concepts.definition: This is the language that creates web pages. Every website you've ever visited uses HTML.
concepts.analogy: The skeleton of a house. HTML provides the structure - the walls, floors, and rooms. Later you'll add paint (CSS) and electricity (JavaScript).

concepts.name: Tags: The building blocks
concepts.definition:
Tags are words inside angle brackets like

and

. They work in pairs: opening tag

, closing tag

, and content between them.
concepts.analogy: Bookends. The opening tag and closing tag hold the content between them.

concepts.name: The basic structure
concepts.definition: Every HTML page has the same skeleton: DOCTYPE, html, head (with title), and body (with visible content).
concepts.analogy: Like the foundation, frame, and rooms of a house - every house needs these basic parts.

understand.keyTakeaway: HTML uses tags in pairs to wrap content. The browser reads these tags and displays your content as a web page.

build.goal: Tell people more about yourself by adding more paragraphs

codeComparison.old:
Hello! I'm [YOUR NAME]

I just created my first web page!
codeComparison.new:
Hello! I'm [YOUR NAME]

I just created my first web page!

I'm learning to code because [YOUR REASON].

My favorite thing to do is [YOUR HOBBY].

I live in [YOUR CITY].

build.hint:
Each

tag creates a new paragraph with space between them. You can add as many as you want!

challenge.title: Create an About Me Page
challenge.mission: Build a complete About Me page with multiple sections using headings and paragraphs.
requirements: Main heading with your name (h1)
At least 3 section headings (h2)
At least 6 paragraphs of content
Sections could include: About Me, My Goals, My Interests, Why I'm Here
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add an h2 section called 'My Coding Journey' and write about what you've learned so far (even if it's just this lesson!)

capabilities: Create HTML files that open in a browser
Use headings (h1, h2) to organize content
Write paragraphs (p) with text
Understand how opening and closing tags work
Save and refresh to see your changes

bridge.preview: In Lesson 2, you'll add links that let people click to other pages. You'll connect multiple pages together and create your first multi-page website.

accomplishments: Make text clickable so it goes to another page
Connect your pages together with links

do.title: Add a link to another website
steps: Open your my-first-page.html file in VS Code
Add this new paragraph at the end of your <body>, right before </body>
Refresh your browser and click the link!
do.code: Want to learn more about coding? Visit FreeCodeCamp for free tutorials.
do.result: You'll see blue underlined text that you can click. When clicked, it opens FreeCodeCamp in the same browser tab.
do.proofRequired: your web page with the clickable link (the link should be blue and underlined)

concepts.name: The <a> tag (anchor tag)
concepts.definition: This creates clickable links - the foundation of the World Wide Web. 'a' stands for 'anchor' because links anchor different pages together.
concepts.analogy: A portal door. It takes you from one place to another when you step through it (click it).

concepts.name: The href attribute
concepts.definition: href is an attribute - extra information you add inside an opening tag. It tells the link where to go.
concepts.analogy: An address on an envelope. The <a> tag is the envelope, the href is where it's going, and the text is the letter inside.

concepts.name: External vs Internal links
concepts.definition: External links go to another website (https://...). Internal links go to another page on YOUR site (about.html).
concepts.analogy: Like the difference between driving to another city (external) vs. walking to another room in your house (internal).

concepts.name: The anatomy of a link
concepts.definition: A link has three parts: opening tag with href, the clickable text people see, and closing tag.
concepts.analogy: Like a door: the frame (opening tag), the handle you grab (text), and the other side of the frame (closing tag).

understand.keyTakeaway: The <a> tag makes text clickable. The href attribute tells it where to go.

build.goal: Create a second page and link between your two pages

codeComparison.new:
Hello! I'm [YOUR NAME]

Content...

Click here to see my hobbies

My Hobbies

Things I Love to Do

Content about your hobbies...

Back to my main page

build.hint: The filename in href="my-hobbies.html" must EXACTLY match the actual filename. Check for typos!

challenge.title: Build a 3-Page Personal Website
challenge.mission: Create a small website with 3 connected pages and a navigation system.
requirements: 3 HTML pages total: index.html (home), about.html (about you), interests.html (hobbies)
Each page has links to the other two pages at the top AND bottom
Each page has at least one external link (to a website related to your interests)
External links open in new tabs (target="_blank")
Internal links open in the same tab
challenge.starterCode:
Welcome to My Website

About Me | My Interests

Hello!

Welcome to my corner of the internet...

About Me | My Interests
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a 4th page called contact.html with your email, social media links, or other ways to reach you

capabilities: Create clickable links with the <a> tag
Use the href attribute to specify where links go
Link to external websites
Connect multiple HTML pages together
Make links open in new tabs with target="_blank"
Build a multi-page website with navigation

bridge.preview: In Lesson 3, you'll add images to your pages. You'll learn how to display photos, control their size, and make images clickable.

accomplishments: Display an image on your web page

do.title: Add your first image
steps: Find a photo you want to use (or use this practice image: https://images.unsplash.com/photo-1571171637578-41bc2dd41cd2?w=400)
Open your index.html (or my-first-page.html) in VS Code
Add the code below somewhere in your <body>
do.result: You'll see an image appear on your page!
do.proofRequired: your web page with the image visible

concepts.name: The tag
concepts.definition: This displays images on your web page. It's a self-closing tag - no closing </img> needed. It just says 'put an image here.'
concepts.analogy: A picture frame that goes on the wall. You don't open and close a picture frame - you just hang it up.

concepts.name: The src attribute (source)
concepts.definition: Tells the browser WHERE to find the image. Can be a URL (https://...) or a filename (my-photo.jpg) if the image is in the same folder.
concepts.analogy: Like an address telling the delivery person where to pick up a package.

concepts.name: The alt attribute (alternative text)
concepts.definition: Describes what the image shows. Used by screen readers for blind users, shows if image fails to load, helps search engines.
concepts.analogy: Audio description at a museum - tells people who can't see the art what it depicts.

concepts.name: Image paths
concepts.definition: Same folder: src='photo.jpg'. Inside folder: src='images/photo.jpg'. From URL: src='https://site.com/image.jpg'
concepts.analogy: Giving directions: 'right here' vs 'in the kitchen' vs 'at 123 Main Street.'

concepts.name: Width and height attributes
concepts.definition: Control image size in pixels. Usually set only width - height scales automatically to keep proportions.
concepts.analogy: Like telling a printer: make this photo 4 inches wide (height adjusts to match).

understand.keyTakeaway: needs both src (where) and alt (what). No closing tag needed.

build.goal: Make your image a reasonable size instead of HUGE
build.hint: You can use height instead of width, but usually set only ONE. The browser automatically scales the other to keep proportions.

challenge.title: Build a Photo Gallery Page
challenge.mission: Create a new page that displays a gallery of images with captions and links.
requirements: Create a new file called gallery.html
Add at least 4 images (mix of URLs and local files)
Each image has a descriptive alt text
Each image is a reasonable size (width between 200-400)
Add a heading above each image describing what it shows
Make at least 2 images clickable (linking to related pages or websites)
Add navigation links to connect this page to your other pages
challenge.starterCode:
My Photo Gallery

Back to Home

[First Image Title]

[Caption explaining the image]
challenge.bonusChallenge: Make one of your images clickable and link it to a full-size version that opens in a new tab

capabilities: Display images with the tag
Use the src attribute to specify image location
Add alt text for accessibility
Control image size with width attribute
Use images from URLs and local files
Make images clickable by wrapping in <a> tags
Understand image file paths

bridge.preview: In Lesson 4, you'll organize information with lists - bullet points for your hobbies, numbered steps for instructions, and navigation menus.

accomplishments: Make text bold, italic, and highlighted
Use special characters that don't exist on your keyboard

do.title: Make text bold and italic
steps: Save and open in your browser
Look at how each tag changes the appearance
do.code:
Learning Text Formatting

This is normal text.

This is really important text that needs emphasis.

This is emphasized text that's slightly important.

You can also combine them for extra emphasis!

Here's something highlighted like with a yellow marker.

This is normal text, but this part is smaller.
do.result: You'll see text that's bold, italic, highlighted in yellow, and different sizes!
do.proofRequired: all the different text formatting styles

concepts.name: <strong> vs <b>
concepts.definition: <strong> means 'this is important' (semantic). <b> just makes it look bold (visual only). Always use <strong> for important content.
concepts.analogy: The difference between yelling 'FIRE!' (important warning) vs. saying 'fire' in a loud voice (just volume).

concepts.name: <em> vs <i>
concepts.definition: <em> means 'this has emphasis' (semantic). <i> just makes it look italic (visual only). Always use <em> for emphasized content.
concepts.analogy: Stressing a word when speaking vs. just tilting your voice.

concepts.name: Other text formatting tags
concepts.definition: <mark> for highlighting, <small> for fine print, <del> for strikethrough, <sub> for subscript (H₂O), <sup> for superscript (x²).
concepts.analogy: Different highlighter colors and text effects in a word processor.

concepts.name: Line breaks and horizontal rules
concepts.definition:
creates a line break (like pressing Enter).
creates a horizontal line divider.
concepts.analogy: Line breaks in poetry vs. scene breaks in stories (the *** divider).

concepts.name: HTML entities
concepts.definition: Special characters using &code; format. < for <, > for >, & for &, © for ©,   for non-breaking space.
concepts.analogy: Like typing emojis using :smile: codes - special symbols that need special codes.

concepts.name: <div> vs <span>
concepts.definition: <div> is a block container (stacks vertically, takes full width). <span> is inline (flows with text).
concepts.analogy: <div> = box that stacks like Lego blocks. <span> = highlighting words in a sentence.

concepts.name: When to use entities
concepts.definition: Use entities when you need to display <, >, &, or other special characters as text instead of HTML code.
concepts.analogy: Like putting quotes around a word to show you're talking about the word itself.

concepts.name: Semantic vs visual tags
concepts.definition: Semantic tags (<strong>, <em>) have meaning. Visual tags (<b>, <i>) just change appearance. Always prefer semantic.
concepts.analogy: Saying 'stop sign' (meaning) vs. 'red octagon' (appearance only).

understand.keyTakeaway: Use semantic tags (<strong>, <em>) instead of purely visual tags (<b>, <i>). They mean something to screen readers and search engines.

build.goal: Add line breaks and use special characters

codeComparison.new:
My Favorite Quote

"The only way to do great work
is to love what you do."
— Steve Jobs

Special Characters

Use <strong> to make text bold.

Copyright © 2024

Temperature: 72°F

build.hint: < means less-than (<), > means greater-than (>), & means ampersand (&). Start with & and end with semicolon.

challenge.title: Format a Blog Post with Proper Typography
challenge.mission: Create a blog post that uses ALL the text formatting techniques you learned.
requirements: Create typography.html
Use <strong> for important warnings or key points
Use <em> for emphasis in at least 2 places
Use <mark> to highlight a key takeaway

Include a quote with
for line breaks
Use <hr> to separate sections
Include at least 3 HTML entities (©, &, <, >, etc.)
Use <small> for a disclaimer or footnote
Use <sup> for a footnote reference
Use both <div> and <span> appropriately
challenge.starterCode: Published on
challenge.bonusChallenge: Add a <blockquote> with a famous coding quote, and use <code> tags for inline code snippets

capabilities: Use <strong> and <em> for semantic emphasis
Highlight text with <mark>
Create smaller text with <small>
Add line breaks with
Create visual dividers with <hr>
Display special characters with HTML entities (©, <, &, etc.)
Understand the difference between <div> (block) and <span> (inline)
Know when to use semantic tags vs generic containers
Format quotes, code, and typography professionally

bridge.preview: In Lesson 9, you'll add multimedia to your pages — videos, audio, embedded YouTube content, and iframes for maps and other sites.
